title: Activities as practice
subtitle: The "live as if" layer: physical, object-based, daily-life-sized.
excerpt: A reference article for the activity practices in the library. Covers what an activity practice is, why it works, how to pick one, and what to do when resistance shows up. Read once; the activity scripts assume it.
sourceNotes: Activity-as-practice draws on the embodied-cognition literature (the body shapes the mind as much as the mind shapes the body) and the "act as if" tradition in cognitive-behavioural and recovery work. The framing of small daily activities as belief-rehearsal is drawn from Rebecca J Page's MONEY (2025) and MANIFESTING (2025) programs.

practice.whenToUse: Read once before using any activity practice in the library. Re-read when an activity feels pointless or you're not sure why the practice is asking you to do this specific physical thing.

content.text: Activity practices are the "live as if" layer of the library. Small physical things you do daily or weekly that teach the body a new pattern. Leave a twenty-pound note in your wallet for a week. Wear the dress you've been saving for an occasion that hasn't arrived. Put the vision in your sock drawer where you see it daily.

This reading covers what an activity practice is, why a physical action carries the work, how to pick one, and what to do when resistance shows up. The individual activity scripts assume it. They name the object, the action, and the time-frame.

A small, physical, repeated action with a clear intention behind it. The action is daily-life-sized — five minutes, one object, one room. The intention is named once and then the body does the work. Walk past the same banknote sitting untouched in the wallet seventy times in a week and the nervous system stops registering it as scarce.

The object is the carrier. The wallet, the lipstick, the vision board, the kitchen candle. The object holds the intention so you don't have to keep restating it. Seeing the object is the reminder; using the object is the practice.
Why a physical action does the work

Beliefs aren't only held in the mind. They're held in the body — in how you stand when you open the bank app, in whether you reach for the good cup or the chipped one, in what you wear on a Tuesday with no plans. Changing the action changes the body's information about what's true. The mind notices later.

This is why an affirmation alone often does less than an affirmation paired with a daily object. "I am safe with money" said in the morning lands lightly. "I am safe with money" said over a wallet that holds a banknote you've decided not to spend for seven days lands in the body. Same sentence; different anchor.

Pick the activity that matches the belief you are rehearsing. If the belief is "money disappears the moment it arrives," the activity is keeping money in the wallet without touching it. If the belief is "I'm not worth the good things until I've earned them," the activity is using the good thing today — the candle, the lipstick, the towel from the bottom of the cupboard.

Pick something that fits the week you're in. Daily-life-sized. A twenty-pound note works because twenty pounds is the kind of money many wallets can hold for a week without breaking anything. If twenty is the food budget, drop to a fiver. The size doesn't matter; the rehearsal does.
Pick one. Not three. Trying to run three activity practices at once dilutes each one — the body needs the repetition of a single action to learn the new pattern.

Most activity practices have a resistance window — somewhere between day two and day four, you'll want to break the rule. Spend the held banknote. Stop wearing the good dress. Put the vision board back in the drawer. The urge is information, not failure.

When the urge arrives, name it. "The urge to spend it is here. It feels like X." Write the line down if you're a journaller, say it out loud if you're not. Then do the practice anyway. The urge is the old pattern fighting for its place; doing the practice through it is what teaches the body the new pattern.
If the urge is overwhelming, drop to a smaller version of the activity. Half the note instead of the whole one. Three days instead of seven. A smaller true rehearsal beats a bigger one you abandon.

Activity-as-practice draws on the embodied-cognition literature — research showing that the body shapes the mind at least as much as the mind shapes the body. It also draws on the "act as if" tradition in cognitive-behavioural therapy and twelve-step recovery work, where repeating a small action is treated as the first move in changing the belief underneath it. The framing of small daily activities as belief-rehearsal is drawn from Rebecca J Page's MONEY (2025) and MANIFESTING (2025) programs, both of which use object-based practices alongside the tapping and journal work.
